does: This paper introduces Anomaly Preference Optimization, a novel framework for generating realistic and diverse anomalous samples from limited data by reformulating anomaly generation as a preference learning problem. The method employs an implicit preference alignment mechanism that uses real anomalies as positive references, extracting optimization signals from denoising trajectory deviations without human annotation. A Time-Aware Capacity Allocation module dynamically distributes model capacity across the diffusion timeline, prioritizing structural diversity in high-noise phases and fine-grained fidelity in low-noise stages. The approach includes a hierarchical sampling strategy during inference that allows precise control over the coherence-alignment trade-off, balancing generation realism with diversity constraints.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in generative modeling with diffusion models and preference learning paradigms that have emerged in large language model alignment. It responds to fundamental challenges in anomaly detection literature regarding the limited availability of anomalous samples and the difficulty of generating realistic out-of-distribution examples. The paper extends prior work on controlled generation and conditional sampling by introducing preference-based learning signals for anomalies. It connects to trajectory-based optimization methods in diffusion models and combines insights from capacity allocation strategies used in multi-task learning and temporal modeling.
enables: This research enables more robust anomaly detection systems that can generalize better with limited anomalous training data, with applications in industrial defect detection, medical imaging, and security. The preference optimization framework could extend to other conditional generation tasks requiring fine-grained control between fidelity and diversity. The Time-Aware Capacity Allocation mechanism opens directions for optimizing diffusion models across different noise regimes for various downstream tasks. The hierarchical sampling strategy provides a foundation for developing interactive generation systems where users can dynamically adjust generation properties, and the approach could inspire preference-based methods for other data scarcity problems in machine learning.
